news_summary: Advancements in sustainable technology and environmental science are driving innovations in renewable energy, biodiversity understanding, environmental DNA research, and eco-friendly waste recycling.
news_summary_fr: Les progrès réalisés dans les domaines des technologies durables et des sciences de l'environnement stimulent les innovations en matière d'énergies renouvelables, de compréhension de la biodiversité, de recherche sur l'ADN environnemental et de recyclage écologique des déchets.
news_summary_es: Los avances en tecnología sostenible y ciencias ambientales están impulsando innovaciones en el ámbito de las energías renovables, el conocimiento de la biodiversidad, la investigación sobre el ADN ambiental y el reciclaje ecológico de residuos.

articles.title: Autonomous closed-loop framework for reproducible perovskite solar cells
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 14 April 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10482-yAutonomous closed-loop framework for reproducible perovskite solar cells
articles.content: The commercialization of perovskite solar cells is bottlenecked by inefficient, trial-and-error approaches reliant on human expertise in both material discovery and device fabrication (1-3). Here, we introduce an autonomous closed-loop framework that integrates machine learning (ML)-driven material discovery with an automated manufacturing platform. The system employs active learning and quantum modeling to rapidly identify high-performance molecules, while the platform uses Bayesian optimization and symbolic regression in a feedback loop to continuously refine the fabrication process. This integrated approach enabled the discovery of a passivation molecule, 5-(aminomethyl)nicotinonitrile hydroiodide (5ANI), which yielded 0.05 cm² solar cells with a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 27.22% (certified maximum power point tracking (MPPT) efficiency of 27.18%) and 21.4 cm² mini-modules with a PCE of 23.49%. Moreover, the devices exhibited long-term operational stability, retaining 98.7% of their initial efficiency after 1,200 hours of continuous operation under the ISOS-L-1I protocol. Crucially, the automated platform achieved an efficiency reproducibility nearly 5 times that of manual fabrication. This work establishes an automated closed-loop system that synergizes ML-powered discovery with the high-fidelity data from automated manufacturing, setting a benchmark for autonomous discovery and manufacturing in photovoltaics and materials.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant breakthrough in solar cell technology through an autonomous closed-loop framework that integrates machine learning and automated manufacturing. This advancement leads to highly efficient, stable, and reproducible perovskite solar cells, which can accelerate commercialization and adoption of renewable energy, benefiting society and the environment on a broad scale.
articles.personality_title: New automated system boosts efficiency and reliability of perovskite solar cells
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Perovskite solar cells are a promising type of solar technology that could provide cleaner energy. However, making these cells has been slow and difficult because scientists have to try many different materials and methods by hand, which takes a lot of time and skill.

**What happened** – Researchers developed an automated system that combines machine learning (a type of computer program that learns from data) with a robot-like manufacturing platform. This system tests new materials and adjusts the production process on its own, without needing constant human input. Using this method, they discovered a new molecule called 5ANI that helped make perovskite solar cells with an efficiency of 27.22%, which means they convert sunlight into electricity very well. The cells also stayed almost as efficient after 1,200 hours of use, showing they are stable and long-lasting.

**Impact** – This is important because the automated system produces solar cells that are not only very efficient but also made in a way that is much more reliable and repeatable than making them by hand. It means factories could produce high-quality solar panels faster and with less waste. The discovery of the 5ANI molecule also shows how computers can help find better materials quickly.

**What's next step** – The researchers plan to use this automated approach to discover even better materials and improve manufacturing processes further. This could help bring affordable and reliable perovskite solar panels to the market sooner, making solar energy more accessible.

**One-sentence takeaway** – An automated system combining machine learning and robotics has created highly efficient and stable perovskite solar cells with improved reliability, paving the way for faster solar technology development.
articles.personality_title_fr: Un nouveau système automatisé améliore l'efficacité et la fiabilité des cellules solaires à pérovskite
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les cellules solaires à pérovskite sont une technologie prometteuse pour produire une énergie plus propre. Mais leur fabrication est lente et compliquée car les chercheurs doivent tester beaucoup de matériaux et méthodes à la main, ce qui demande du temps et des compétences.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Des chercheurs ont créé un système automatisé qui combine l'apprentissage automatique (un programme informatique qui apprend des données) avec une plateforme de fabrication robotisée. Ce système teste de nouveaux matériaux et ajuste la production tout seul, sans intervention humaine constante. Grâce à cette méthode, ils ont découvert une nouvelle molécule appelée 5ANI qui a permis de fabriquer des cellules solaires à pérovskite avec une efficacité de 27,22 %, c’est-à-dire qu’elles transforment très bien la lumière du soleil en électricité. Ces cellules ont aussi gardé presque toute leur efficacité après 1 200 heures d’utilisation, montrant qu’elles sont stables et durables.

**Impact** – C’est important car ce système automatisé produit des cellules solaires très efficaces et fabriquées de manière beaucoup plus fiable et reproductible que la fabrication manuelle. Cela signifie que les usines pourraient produire des panneaux solaires de haute qualité plus rapidement et avec moins de déchets. La découverte de la molécule 5ANI montre aussi comment les ordinateurs peuvent aider à trouver rapidement de meilleurs matériaux.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’utiliser cette méthode automatisée pour découvrir encore de meilleurs matériaux et améliorer la fabrication. Cela pourrait permettre de commercialiser plus vite des panneaux solaires à pérovskite abordables et fiables, rendant l’énergie solaire plus accessible.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Un système automatisé combinant apprentissage automatique et robotique a créé des cellules solaires à pérovskite très efficaces et stables avec une meilleure fiabilité, ouvrant la voie à un développement plus rapide des technologies solaires.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo sistema automatizado mejora la eficiencia y confiabilidad de las células solares de perovskita
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Las células solares de perovskita son una tecnología prometedora para producir energía más limpia. Sin embargo, su fabricación es lenta y complicada porque los científicos deben probar muchos materiales y métodos a mano, lo que toma mucho tiempo y habilidad.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores desarrollaron un sistema automatizado que combina aprendizaje automático (un tipo de programa informático que aprende de datos) con una plataforma de fabricación robótica. Este sistema prueba nuevos materiales y ajusta el proceso de producción por sí solo, sin necesitar intervención humana constante. Usando este método, descubrieron una nueva molécula llamada 5ANI que ayudó a fabricar células solares de perovskita con una eficiencia del 27,22 %, lo que significa que convierten la luz solar en electricidad muy bien. Las células también mantuvieron casi la misma eficiencia después de 1.200 horas de uso, mostrando que son estables y duraderas.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque el sistema automatizado produce células solares que no solo son muy eficientes, sino que también se fabrican de una manera mucho más confiable y repetible que al hacerlas a mano. Esto significa que las fábricas podrían producir paneles solares de alta calidad más rápido y con menos desperdicio. El descubrimiento de la molécula 5ANI también muestra cómo las computadoras pueden ayudar a encontrar mejores materiales rápidamente.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean usar este enfoque automatizado para descubrir materiales aún mejores y mejorar los procesos de fabricación. Esto podría ayudar a llevar paneles solares de perovskita asequibles y confiables al mercado más pronto, haciendo la energía solar más accesible.

**Conclusión en una frase** – Un sistema automatizado que combina aprendizaje automático y robótica ha creado células solares de perovskita muy eficientes y estables con mayor confiabilidad, allanando el camino para un desarrollo más rápido de la tecnología solar.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a glowing, intricately patterned solar cell panel seamlessly merging with a futuristic, glowing robotic arm gently assembling molecular structures represented by geometric, crystalline shapes, all set against a softly lit abstract background symbolizing a continuous feedback loop of innovation and precision.

articles.title: Scientists just debunked a 50-year myth about Hawaii’s birds
articles.summary: A new study from the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa is overturning a decades-old belief that Indigenous Hawaiians hunted native waterbirds to extinction. Instead, researchers found no scientific evidence supporting this claim and propose a more complex explanation involving climate change, invasive species, and shifts in land use—many occurring before Polynesian arrival or after traditional stewardship systems were disrupted.
articles.content:
A new study from the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa is reshaping a decades-old belief about the disappearance of Hawaiʻi's native waterbirds. Researchers report that there is no scientific evidence showing Indigenous People hunted these species to extinction. Published in the journal Ecosphere, the study disputes this widely accepted narrative and introduces a broader explanation for why the birds declined.

The research team found no signs of widespread overhunting. Instead, they point to a combination of factors, including climate change, invasive species, and shifts in land use. Many of these changes occurred either before Polynesians arrived in Hawaiʻi or after traditional Indigenous land management practices were disrupted. The study also suggests that several waterbird species now considered endangered may have reached their highest numbers just before European contact, when wetland management was a central part of Kānaka ʻŌiwi (Native Hawaiian) society.

Rethinking Conservation Assumptions

"So much of science is biased by the notion that humans are inevitable agents of ecocide, and we destroy nature wherever we go. This idea has shaped the dominant narrative in conservation, which automatically places the blame for extinctions on the first people -- the Indigenous People -- of a place. Even where there is zero scientific evidence to support it, the myth of Hawaiians hunting birds to extinctions took root in Hawaiʻi and for decades has been taught as if it was a scientific fact," shares Kawika Winter, associate professor at UH Mānoa Hawaiʻi Institute of Marine Biology (HIMB), director of the Heʻeia National Estuarine Research Reserve (NERR), and co-author of the paper. "Our study not only dispels this myth, but also contributes to a growing body of evidence that Indigenous stewardship represents the best ways for native birds to thrive in a world where humans are not going away."

The study revisits existing data while setting aside a common assumption that humans are inherently harmful to natural systems. By doing so, it offers a more detailed and balanced view of ecological history and highlights the need for more careful interpretation in conservation science.

"Science has matured to a point where graduate students are being trained to challenge its own long-standing world view," notes Kristen Harmon, lead author on the paper who recently earned a PhD from UH Mānoa College of Tropical Agriculture and Human Resilience (CTAHR) Department of Natural Resources and Environmental Management. "Our interpretation of historical ecology, how ecological systems change over time, influences our approaches to solving global-scale ecological problems. Bringing together information from different disciplines and knowledge systems can yield a more accurate picture of reality, which is ultimately the goal of every scientist."

Indigenous Stewardship and Bird Recovery

The findings could play an important role in shaping conservation strategies across Hawaiʻi, especially for endangered waterbirds such as ʻalae ʻula (Gallinula chloropus) and ʻaeʻo (Himantopus mexicanus knudseni). Researchers say that restoring traditional systems may be key to helping these species recover.

"Recent studies support what Hawaiians have always known -- that restoration of loʻi (wetland agro-ecosystems) is critically important to bring these waterbirds into abundance again," said Melissa Price, an Associate Professor who runs the Wildlife Ecology Lab at CTAHR. "If we wish to transform our islands from the 'Extinction Capital of the World' into the 'Recovery Capital of the World,' we need to restore relationships between nature and communities."

This updated understanding may also help resolve tensions that have existed between conservation groups and Native Hawaiian communities, opening the door to more inclusive approaches.

Ulalia Woodside Lee, who was not a part of this research project, offered some reflections as the Hawai'i and Palmyra Executive Director for The Nature Conservancy, "For generations, Native Hawaiians have been criticized for causing the extinctions of our precious native birds. This has contributed to a breakdown in trust between the Hawaiian community and conservationists, and the exclusion of Native Hawaiians from important conservation decisions. This study will help us to move past those untruths, so that we can all move together into a brighter future where our native species are thriving again."
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific study that overturns a long-standing myth blaming Indigenous Hawaiians for native bird extinctions, instead highlighting the role of climate change, invasive species, and disrupted stewardship. This has broad implications for conservation strategies, Indigenous rights, and ecological restoration in Hawaiʻi, promoting more inclusive and effective environmental management with potential large-scale benefits for native species recovery and community relations.
articles.personality_title: New study clears Indigenous Hawaiians of blame for native bird losses
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – For decades, many people believed that Indigenous Hawaiians hunted native waterbirds to extinction. This idea shaped how scientists and conservationists thought about bird declines in Hawaiʻi.

**What happened** – Researchers at the University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoa studied this claim and found no scientific proof that Indigenous Hawaiians caused these extinctions by hunting. Instead, they discovered that factors like climate change, invasive species, and changes in land use were the main reasons for bird declines. Some of these changes happened before Polynesians arrived or after traditional land care was disrupted.

**Impact** – This study changes how we understand Hawaiʻi’s ecological history. It shows that Indigenous people managed the land in ways that helped birds thrive, especially before European contact. This challenges old ideas that blamed Indigenous people unfairly and opens the door to including their knowledge in conservation efforts.

**What’s next step** – Conservation groups in Hawaiʻi may now focus on restoring traditional wetland farming systems, known as loʻi, to help endangered waterbirds recover. This new understanding could improve cooperation between Native Hawaiians and conservationists, leading to better protection for native species.

**One-sentence takeaway** – New research shows that climate and environmental changes—not Indigenous hunting—caused native Hawaiian waterbird declines, highlighting the importance of traditional stewardship in conservation.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle étude disculpe les Hawaïens autochtones pour la disparition des oiseaux natifs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Pendant des décennies, beaucoup ont pensé que les Hawaïens autochtones avaient chassé jusqu’à l’extinction les oiseaux aquatiques natifs. Cette idée a influencé la vision scientifique sur la baisse des populations d’oiseaux à Hawaï.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de l’Université d’Hawaï à Mānoa ont étudié cette affirmation et n’ont trouvé aucune preuve scientifique que les Hawaïens autochtones aient causé ces extinctions par la chasse. Ils ont plutôt découvert que le changement climatique, les espèces envahissantes et les modifications de l’utilisation des terres étaient les principales causes du déclin des oiseaux. Certaines de ces modifications ont eu lieu avant l’arrivée des Polynésiens ou après la perturbation des pratiques traditionnelles.

**Impact** – Cette étude change notre compréhension de l’histoire écologique d’Hawaï. Elle montre que les peuples autochtones géraient la terre de manière à favoriser la survie des oiseaux, surtout avant le contact européen. Cela remet en cause les idées anciennes qui les blâmaient injustement et ouvre la voie à l’intégration de leurs savoirs dans la conservation.

**Prochaine étape** – Les groupes de conservation à Hawaï pourraient désormais se concentrer sur la restauration des systèmes traditionnels de culture en terrasses humides, appelés loʻi, pour aider à la récupération des oiseaux aquatiques en danger. Cette nouvelle compréhension pourrait améliorer la coopération entre les Hawaïens autochtones et les écologistes, pour une meilleure protection des espèces natives.

**Résumé en une phrase** – De nouvelles recherches montrent que les changements climatiques et environnementaux – pas la chasse autochtone – ont causé le déclin des oiseaux aquatiques hawaïens, soulignant l’importance de la gestion traditionnelle dans la conservation.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo estudio libera a los indígenas hawaianos de culpa por la pérdida de aves nativas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Durante décadas, se creyó que los indígenas hawaianos cazaron hasta la extinción a las aves acuáticas nativas. Esta idea influyó en cómo científicos y conservacionistas entendían la disminución de aves en Hawai.

Qué pasó – Investigadores de la Universidad de Hawaiʻi en Mānoa analizaron esta afirmación y no encontraron pruebas científicas de que los indígenas hawaianos causaran estas extinciones por caza. En cambio, descubrieron que el cambio climático, especies invasoras y cambios en el uso de la tierra fueron las principales causas del descenso de las aves. Algunos de estos cambios ocurrieron antes de la llegada de los polinesios o después de que se interrumpieran las prácticas tradicionales de cuidado de la tierra.

Impacto – Este estudio cambia la forma en que entendemos la historia ecológica de Hawai. Muestra que los pueblos indígenas manejaban la tierra para ayudar a que las aves prosperaran, especialmente antes del contacto europeo. Esto desafía ideas antiguas que culpaban injustamente a los indígenas y abre la puerta a incluir sus conocimientos en la conservación.

Próximo paso – Los grupos de conservación en Hawai podrían ahora enfocarse en restaurar los sistemas tradicionales de agricultura en humedales, llamados loʻi, para ayudar a que las aves acuáticas en peligro se recuperen. Este nuevo entendimiento podría mejorar la cooperación entre los hawaianos indígenas y los conservacionistas, para una mejor protección de las especies nativas.

Conclusión en una frase – Una nueva investigación muestra que los cambios climáticos y ambientales, no la caza indígena, causaron el declive de aves acuáticas hawaianas, destacando la importancia del manejo tradicional en la conservación.
articles.image_prompt: A serene Hawaiian wetland at dawn, softly illuminated with warm golden light, featuring stylized native waterbirds like the ʻalae ʻula and ʻaeʻo gracefully wading among restored loʻi kalo (taro terraces), with subtle abstract patterns symbolizing Indigenous stewardship gently weaving through the landscape, all rendered in delicate, natural earth tones and soft greens.

articles.title: The air is full of DNA — here’s what scientists are using it for
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 14 April 2026; doi:10.1038/d41586-026-01099-2Airborne genetic material can be used to paint a picture of ecosystem health, watch for invasive species and even identify humans.
articles.content:
Ryan Kelly is in awe of what floats invisibly in the air.

“It is completely mind-blowing,” says Kelly, who studies environmental DNA (eDNA) at the University of Washington in Seattle. “We are absolutely surrounded by information in the form of DNA and RNA, at all times.”

Rare bird’s detection highlights promise of ‘environmental DNA’

Scientists have long pulled DNA from water and soil, but they have only just started to see the air as a source of genetic information. Over the past decade or so, researchers have been learning how to measure airborne DNA, study its abundance and use it to put together a picture of an ecosystem’s inhabitants and health. Airborne DNA is being used to monitor individual species, and being trialled as a way to detect invasive species or attacks with biological weapons. It is also being tested as a way to judge the success of conservation efforts.

The technique promises to link “the whole [of] biodiversity, the whole world together with a single assay that’s really rapid and that can even be done in the field and analysed in the cloud”, says David Duffy, a researcher who specializes in wildlife disease genomics at the University of Florida in St Augustine.

But there is still a lot to pin down, such as how fast DNA decays in the air and how far it travels. Some genetic material pulled from the air comes from humans, and several scientists are concerned that when using the technique for conservation research, it could inadvertently reveal people’s ethnicity or whether a person has a genetic disorder — and even be used to identify individuals.

Clouds of DNA

Scratch your head and you’ll release DNA-rich cellular material into the air. There, it will mingle with DNA from myriad other sources: your own and others’ exhalations and exfoliations, fragments of hair, feathers, excrement, pollen and spores, and microorganisms such as viruses and microalgae. This DNA, which can include segments that are tens of thousands of base pairs long, will then wander the air for perhaps a few days, often clinging to dust particles. It can travel distances that range from a few metres to several thousand.

Although eDNA is already collected routinely from water, snow and soil, to gather information about biodiversity or to track contaminants or viruses, scientists have not typically monitored sources of DNA in air other than pollen and spores — robust packages designed to travel on the breeze.

But, in the early 2010s, various ecologists began to wonder whether air might contain useful DNA traces beyond those wrapped in such windborne bundles. In 2013, biologists Matt Clark at the Natural History Museum in London and Richard Leggett at the Earlham Institute in Norwich, UK, took air samples in a greenhouse and outside it.

“We were just wondering whether we would get anything,” says Clark. “Actually, we got dozens — hundreds — of things turning up.”

Meanwhile, at Texas Tech University in Lubbock, ecologist Matthew Barnes analysed air samples using techniques developed for collecting waterborne eDNA, and discovered they were teeming with DNA from leaves and flowers, as well as types of pollen not designed to be windborne. He realized then the potential for understanding whole plant communities using air1.

But it was the discovery of tiger DNA near Cambridge, UK, that alerted the wider community to airborne DNA’s potential. Elizabeth Clare at York University in Toronto, Canada, and Joanne Littlefair at University College London wanted to know whether they could find animal DNA in the air. They collected samples at a small zoo in Cambridgeshire, UK, reasoning that they would know the origin of any DNA they found, because the exotic animals were confined to the park.

In the laboratory, the researchers extracted the DNA from the samples, and amplified and sequenced it. They found that they could sniff out tigers 200 metres away from their enclosure, as well as many of the zoo’s other animals, their food — including chicken, horse and pig — and wildlife such as hedgehogs, bats and squirrels. In total, the samples contained DNA from 25 species of mammal and bird, including 17 kept at the zoo2. Another study near Copenhagen Zoo, published at the same time, had similar findings3.

“Airborne animal DNA has always been there, it’s just that we’ve never looked for it,” says Simon Creer, who studies molecular ecology at Bangor University, UK.

Technicians collect fungal spores from the air in central Finland.Credit:Tommi Sassi, University of Jyväskylä

But it was a physicist who found a way to scale the method up. James Allerton, at the National Physical Laboratory in London, suggested that Clare examine samples taken by the UK Heavy Metals monitoring network, which has 25 air pumps, located in cities, in the countryside and at industrial sites.

The researchers studied samples from 15 of the network’s sites and, last year, published4 what they say is the world’s first national survey of terrestrial biodiversity using airborne eDNA. They found common UK animals, as well as exotic pets such as parrots and an invasive fish species, the silver carp (Hypophthalmichthys molitrix), that had not previously been reported in the region. From vertebrates to single-celled protists, they picked up 1,100 taxa.

To check the reliability of their method, the researchers compared their results with data from massive databases such as iNaturalist, in which citizen scientists record what they see. iNaturalist had failed to pick up half of what the team found. In turn, eDNA did not reflect 43% of the iNaturalist observations. Citizen science tended to find more birds and other charismatic, visible species near human habitation. Airborne DNA picked up more of the small, the invisible and the nocturnal, including fungi, lichens, invertebrates and plants other than trees, says Littlefair. “These are really the powerhouses of ecosystem function.”

The method, says the team, is “a realistic solution to monitoring the dynamics of life on land”. Now, the researchers are helping countries with similar monitoring networks to do the same.

Archives of air

But what if you could harness a network that pumps huge amounts of air through its filters, and that has records stretching back decades? In 2015, molecular biologist Per Stenberg at Umeå University in Sweden heard about just such a possibility — a 70-year history of biodiversity, told in wisps of DNA caught on tens of thousands of filters and stored at the Swedish Defence Research Agency in Stockholm.

He was at a seminar about Sweden’s radionuclide-detection network, built in the late 1950s to detect nuclear-weapons tests. The 25 stations suck in hundreds of cubic metres of air per hour and the contents are then stored on glass-fibre filters.

Stenberg set about analysing the filters from a station north of the Arctic Circle. Whereas Littlefair’s team searched for short marker regions of DNA that identify individual species — known as DNA metabarcoding — Stenberg used shotgun sequencing, in which DNA is broken down into tiny pieces, sequenced and matched to known reference genomes using a computer. The shotgun approach consumes more time and energy and requires more-complex statistical techniques than does the metabarcoding technique. But the results are more detailed.

It was four years before he and his collaborator Mats Forsman, the agency’s research director, got results5.

“Viruses, bacteria, fungi, plants, animals, birds, fish … the intestinal parasites of moose,” recounts Stenberg. “I mean, whatever was out there and had a reference to match it, we could see — every single organism that is not extremely rare in the ecosystem.”

The results indicated that the technique could be trusted, he says. “So then it was like: wow. This is something we need to explore.”

Accidental DNA collection by air sensors could revolutionize wildlife tracking

Ecologists are doing just that, documenting weekly, seasonal and cyclic fluctuations in the abundance of many species and matching these to climate variations. They have uncovered long-term community changes — the rise and fall in the abundance of pine trees because of changing forestry management, and a concomitant decline in other trees, mosses, lichens and fungi. They have tracked over time well-known co-variations between several species, such as those between flies and some bacteria, and found new ones.

Europe is dotted with radionuclide-detection stations, which could provide “an unprecedented opportunity to reconstruct ecological history and detect ongoing changes”, say Stenberg and his co-authors.

Such networks are, however, in fixed locations. Some scientists are experimenting with more flexible monitoring. Erin Hahn, who studies conservation genetics at the Australian National Wildlife Collection in Canberra, has designed and 3D-printed passive samplers, which don’t need an energy supply, and given them to landholders across New South Wales.

Her team is still at the pilot stage. “There’s heaps of variables around airflow, light exposure, proximity to game trails,” says Hahn. “We’re just starting to chip away at them to better understand how DNA moves around.” What Hahn ultimately wants is a nimble network that can pinpoint change quickly, flagging invasive species or crashing populations that need management.

Total read-out

For governments, companies, scientists and conservationists aiming to track the health of ecosystems, airborne DNA could provide a comprehensive, regular read-out of biodiversity on land.

“It means that we can rapidly assess environments before, during and after mitigation, and not just think we improve biodiversity, but really have a quantitative measure,” says Duffy, who is evaluating its potential for tracking forest restoration.

Air-sampling devices on a beach in Norfolk, UK, gather material for DNA sequencing.Credit: DarrenHeavens/Earlham Institute

DNA read-outs could also help to chronicle ecosystem vitality, by tracking pathogen load and the genetic diversity of individual species — an indicator of health.

There are other enduring ecological questions that airborne DNA could help to solve. Stenberg’s group is developing models that aim to understand cause and effect in ecosystems.

“We know that foxes eat rabbits, and rabbits eat some plants and so on,” Stenberg says. “But the full ecosystem — when we talk about the bacteria, the nematodes, the insects, the plants, the animals — we basically have no idea.” Uncovering more detail could provide practical information on how ecosystems respond to damage.

Hard to interpret

But there’s a lot of troubleshooting to do first.
articles.reasoning: The article describes a significant scientific breakthrough in using airborne environmental DNA (eDNA) to monitor biodiversity and ecosystem health on a large scale. This technique offers a rapid, comprehensive, and non-invasive method to track species, detect invasive organisms, and assess conservation efforts, which has broad environmental and societal benefits. The story is well-detailed, focusing on the development, applications, and potential of airborne eDNA, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news with meaningful real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: Scientists track wildlife and ecosystems using DNA floating in the air
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have traditionally collected DNA from water and soil to study living things and their environments. Recently, they discovered that DNA also travels through the air, carried by tiny particles like dust, pollen, and skin cells. This airborne DNA can reveal which plants, animals, and microbes live in an area.

**What happened** – Researchers from several universities and institutes started collecting air samples using special devices. They found DNA from many species, including rare animals like tigers near zoos and invasive fish in the wild. One team even used old air filters stored for decades to study how ecosystems changed over time. These studies show that air contains a rich mix of DNA that scientists can use to monitor biodiversity and track changes in nature.

**Impact** – This method is important because it gives a fast and non-invasive way to check the health of ecosystems. It can detect small or hidden species that people might miss, such as fungi or tiny insects. Governments and conservation groups can use airborne DNA to watch for invasive species, measure the success of restoration projects, and better understand how ecosystems work together. It also opens new possibilities for studying long-term environmental changes using stored air samples.

**What's next step** – Scientists want to improve how they collect and analyze airborne DNA. They are working on portable samplers and computer models to better understand how DNA moves through the air. Expanding this technology could lead to networks that quickly detect changes in wildlife populations and alert authorities to environmental problems. Researchers are also exploring how to protect people’s privacy when human DNA appears in air samples.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Airborne DNA offers a powerful new way to monitor nature by capturing the genetic traces of plants and animals floating invisibly in the air.
articles.personality_title_fr: Les scientifiques suivent la faune et les écosystèmes grâce à l’ADN présent dans l’air
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Jusqu’à présent, les scientifiques collectaient l’ADN dans l’eau et le sol pour étudier les êtres vivants et leurs environnements. Récemment, ils ont découvert que l’ADN voyage aussi dans l’air, transporté par de minuscules particules comme la poussière, le pollen ou les cellules de peau. Cet ADN aérien peut révéler quelles plantes, animaux et microbes vivent dans une zone.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs de plusieurs universités et instituts ont commencé à prélever des échantillons d’air avec des appareils spéciaux. Ils ont trouvé de l’ADN de nombreuses espèces, y compris des animaux rares comme des tigres près des zoos et des poissons invasifs dans la nature. Une équipe a même utilisé de vieux filtres d’air stockés depuis des décennies pour étudier l’évolution des écosystèmes. Ces études montrent que l’air contient un mélange riche d’ADN que les scientifiques peuvent utiliser pour surveiller la biodiversité et suivre les changements dans la nature.

**Impact** – Cette méthode est importante car elle offre un moyen rapide et non invasif de vérifier la santé des écosystèmes. Elle peut détecter des espèces petites ou cachées que les humains pourraient manquer, comme des champignons ou de petits insectes. Les gouvernements et les groupes de conservation peuvent utiliser l’ADN aérien pour repérer des espèces invasives, mesurer le succès des projets de restauration, et mieux comprendre le fonctionnement des écosystèmes. Cela ouvre aussi de nouvelles possibilités pour étudier les changements environnementaux sur le long terme grâce aux échantillons d’air conservés.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques veulent améliorer la collecte et l’analyse de l’ADN aérien. Ils travaillent sur des capteurs portables et des modèles informatiques pour mieux comprendre comment l’ADN se déplace dans l’air. Étendre cette technologie pourrait créer des réseaux capables de détecter rapidement des changements dans les populations animales et d’alerter les autorités en cas de problème environnemental. Les chercheurs étudient aussi comment protéger la vie privée lorsque l’ADN humain apparaît dans les échantillons d’air.

**Résumé en une phrase** – L’ADN présent dans l’air offre une nouvelle méthode puissante pour surveiller la nature en capturant les traces génétiques des plantes et des animaux flottant invisiblement dans l’air.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos rastrean la vida silvestre y los ecosistemas usando ADN en el aire
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos solían recolectar ADN del agua y el suelo para estudiar los seres vivos y sus ambientes. Recientemente, descubrieron que el ADN también viaja por el aire, transportado por pequeñas partículas como polvo, polen y células de piel. Este ADN en el aire puede mostrar qué plantas, animales y microbios viven en un lugar.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de varias universidades e institutos comenzaron a tomar muestras de aire con dispositivos especiales. Encontraron ADN de muchas especies, incluyendo animales raros como tigres cerca de zoológicos y peces invasores en la naturaleza. Un equipo incluso usó filtros de aire antiguos guardados durante décadas para estudiar cómo cambiaron los ecosistemas con el tiempo. Estos estudios muestran que el aire contiene una mezcla rica de ADN que los científicos pueden usar para monitorear la biodiversidad y seguir los cambios en la naturaleza.

**Impacto** – Este método es importante porque ofrece una forma rápida y sin daño para revisar la salud de los ecosistemas. Puede detectar especies pequeñas o escondidas que las personas podrían no ver, como hongos o insectos diminutos. Los gobiernos y grupos de conservación pueden usar el ADN aéreo para detectar especies invasoras, medir el éxito de proyectos de restauración y entender mejor cómo funcionan los ecosistemas. También abre nuevas posibilidades para estudiar cambios ambientales a largo plazo usando muestras de aire almacenadas.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos quieren mejorar cómo recolectan y analizan el ADN en el aire. Están trabajando en muestreadores portátiles y modelos por computadora para entender mejor cómo se mueve el ADN por el aire. Ampliar esta tecnología podría crear redes que detecten rápidamente cambios en las poblaciones de animales y alerten a las autoridades sobre problemas ambientales. Los investigadores también exploran cómo proteger la privacidad cuando aparece ADN humano en las muestras de aire.

**Frase clave** – El ADN en el aire ofrece una forma poderosa de monitorear la naturaleza al capturar las huellas genéticas de plantas y animales que flotan invisiblemente en el aire.
articles.image_prompt: A softly glowing cloud of delicate DNA strands and microscopic particles gently swirling above a serene natural landscape, with subtle outlines of diverse animals and plants emerging from the mist, all rendered in warm, earthy tones with fine, detailed brushstrokes.

articles.title: UK startup turns festival urine into forest-grade fertilizer
articles.summary:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Only seven percent of Britain’s native woodlands are in good condition. Pests, pathogens, and invasive species have worked through the rest. And rising fertilizer costs, driven by ongoing conflict, have not helped. A Bristol-based startup thinks part of the answer has been sitting in festival portable toilets all along. […]
The post UK startup turns festival urine into forest-grade fertilizer first appeared on The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News.
articles.content:
BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM

Only seven percent of Britain’s native woodlands are in good condition. Pests, pathogens, and invasive species have worked through the rest. And rising fertilizer costs, driven by ongoing conflict, have not helped. A Bristol-based startup thinks part of the answer has been sitting in festival portable toilets all along.

NPK Recovery, based at the University of the West of England, collects urine at large events such as the London Marathon and Boomtown Festival, and processes it into plant fertilizer on-site. The company removes contaminants from raw urine and concentrates the nitrogen, phosphorus, and potassium that plants need. Field trials show the product performs comparably to synthetic alternatives. Researchers are also quick to note that it doesn’t smell.

The company is now taking on its biggest test: a three-year trial to grow 4,500 native British trees using urine-derived fertilizer, funded by a £435,627 (approximately $553,000) Forestry Commission grant.

From festival grounds to a Welsh forest

The trees (beech, Scots pine, and other native species) will be grown at a nursery outside Abergavenny in south Wales, run by Stump up for Trees, a farmer-led charity that recently planted its 500,000th tree and is working toward one million. Once established, they’ll go into Bannau Brycheiniog National Park, also known as the Brecon Beacons.

This is the first time urine-based fertilizer has been used in UK tree cultivation. The Forestry Commission is funding the trial, a sign that interest in fertilizer alternatives is moving beyond the fringes.

Co-founder Lucy Bell-Reeves called it “a circular solution that can revitalise our struggling native species.” She likes the thought that festival crowds who contributed to the process will have helped create “a fledgling Welsh forest, which could flourish for hundreds of years.”

The science behind the smell-free product

Urine contains the core nutrients plants need, but it also carries contaminants that make raw application impractical for agriculture or forestry. NPK Recovery’s process strips those out, producing a concentrated nutrient solution that can be applied at scale. “We need to stop flushing crop and tree-growing nutrients down the loo, and start using them to increase our fertilizer security,” Bell-Reeves said. “After all, we’re not about to run out of urine any time soon.”

Research and development scientist Olivia Wilson said the goal goes beyond this one project. With imported fertilizer prices still elevated, she said the company hopes “to provide a bit of fertilizer security for growers in the UK who are looking for a more sustainable source for their fertilizer needs.”

Author and journalist Rob Penn, who co-founded Stump up for Trees, called the project “ground-breaking” with implications for the future of sustainable forestry.

Over the next three years, researchers will track growth rates, compare results against synthetic fertilizer plots, and test whether the approach holds across different tree species and soil types. A lot rides on whether the trees actually thrive… but if they do, it becomes much harder to justify flushing all those nutrients away.

Did this solution stand out? Share it with a friend or support our mission by becoming an Emissary.
articles.source: The Optimist Daily: Making Solutions the News
articles.reasoning: The article reports on a UK startup innovatively recycling human urine from festivals into effective, smell-free fertilizer to restore native woodlands, addressing environmental degradation and rising fertilizer costs. The project has broad ecological significance, is supported by a major forestry grant, and includes a large-scale, multi-year trial with measurable impact on forest restoration, making it a substantial and inspiring environmental breakthrough.
articles.personality_title: UK startup turns festival urine into fertilizer to grow native trees
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Only seven percent of Britain’s native woodlands are in good shape. Many trees are harmed by pests, diseases, and invasive plants. At the same time, fertilizer prices have gone up a lot, making it harder to grow healthy forests.

**What happened** – A company called NPK Recovery, based in Bristol, collects urine from big events like the London Marathon and Boomtown Festival. They clean and process this urine on-site to make fertilizer that does not smell and helps plants grow. Now, with a grant from the UK Forestry Commission, they are starting a three-year project to use this urine-based fertilizer to grow 4,500 native trees in Wales. The trees include beech and Scots pine and will later be planted in the Brecon Beacons National Park.

**Impact** – This is the first time urine-based fertilizer is being tested for growing trees in the UK. It offers a new way to recycle valuable plant nutrients instead of wasting them by flushing them away. The project could help protect native forests and reduce reliance on expensive synthetic fertilizers. If successful, it shows a creative way to support forest growth using resources people already produce.

**What's next step** – Over the next three years, scientists will watch how well the trees grow with the urine fertilizer compared to regular fertilizer. They will test different tree types and soils to see if this method can work widely. The results will help decide if this approach can become a regular part of forest care in the UK.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A UK startup is turning festival urine into clean, effective fertilizer to help grow native trees and support forest restoration.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une startup britannique transforme l'urine des festivals en engrais pour les arbres natifs
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Seulement sept pour cent des forêts indigènes britanniques sont en bon état. Beaucoup d'arbres sont affectés par des parasites, des maladies et des espèces envahissantes. En même temps, le prix des engrais a beaucoup augmenté, rendant la croissance des forêts plus difficile.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Une entreprise appelée NPK Recovery, basée à Bristol, collecte l'urine lors de grands événements comme le marathon de Londres et le festival Boomtown. Ils nettoient et transforment cette urine sur place en engrais sans odeur qui aide les plantes à pousser. Maintenant, avec une subvention de la Forestry Commission britannique, ils lancent un projet de trois ans pour utiliser cet engrais à base d'urine afin de faire pousser 4 500 arbres indigènes au Pays de Galles. Les arbres comprennent le hêtre et le pin sylvestre et seront ensuite plantés dans le parc national des Brecon Beacons.

**Impact** – C’est la première fois que l’engrais à base d’urine est testé pour la culture d’arbres au Royaume-Uni. Cela offre une nouvelle façon de recycler les nutriments précieux au lieu de les jeter. Le projet pourrait aider à protéger les forêts indigènes et réduire la dépendance aux engrais synthétiques coûteux. S’il réussit, il montrera une méthode créative pour soutenir la croissance des forêts avec des ressources déjà produites par les gens.

**Prochaine étape** – Pendant les trois prochaines années, les scientifiques observeront la croissance des arbres avec cet engrais comparé à l’engrais classique. Ils testeront différents types d’arbres et de sols pour voir si cette méthode peut fonctionner largement. Les résultats aideront à décider si cette approche peut devenir une partie régulière des soins forestiers au Royaume-Uni.

**En une phrase** – Une startup britannique transforme l’urine des festivals en engrais propre et efficace pour aider à faire pousser des arbres indigènes et restaurer les forêts.
articles.personality_title_es: Startup del Reino Unido convierte la orina de festivales en fertilizante para árboles nativos
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Solo el siete por ciento de los bosques nativos de Gran Bretaña están en buen estado. Muchos árboles están dañados por plagas, enfermedades y especies invasoras. Al mismo tiempo, los precios de los fertilizantes han subido mucho, lo que dificulta el crecimiento de los bosques.

**Qué pasó** – Una empresa llamada NPK Recovery, con sede en Bristol, recoge orina en grandes eventos como el Maratón de Londres y el Festival Boomtown. Limpian y procesan esta orina en el lugar para hacer un fertilizante sin olor que ayuda a las plantas a crecer. Ahora, con una subvención de la Comisión Forestal del Reino Unido, están comenzando un proyecto de tres años para usar este fertilizante hecho con orina para cultivar 4,500 árboles nativos en Gales. Los árboles incluyen haya y pino silvestre y luego serán plantados en el Parque Nacional Brecon Beacons.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se prueba un fertilizante a base de orina para cultivar árboles en el Reino Unido. Ofrece una nueva manera de reciclar nutrientes valiosos en lugar de desperdiciarlos. El proyecto podría ayudar a proteger los bosques nativos y reducir la dependencia de fertilizantes sintéticos caros. Si tiene éxito, mostrará una forma creativa de apoyar el crecimiento forestal usando recursos que las personas ya producen.

**Qué sigue** – Durante los próximos tres años, los científicos observarán qué tan bien crecen los árboles con este fertilizante comparado con el fertilizante común. Probarán diferentes tipos de árboles y suelos para ver si este método puede funcionar en general. Los resultados ayudarán a decidir si este enfoque puede convertirse en parte regular del cuidado forestal en el Reino Unido.

**Una frase clave** – Una startup del Reino Unido convierte la orina de festivales en fertilizante limpio y efectivo para ayudar a cultivar árboles nativos y restaurar bosques.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a vibrant Welsh forest nursery where young beech and Scots pine saplings grow healthily, with subtle, stylized festival tents and portable toilets in the distant background blending gently into the landscape, connected by a flowing, glowing stream symbolizing purified nutrients circulating from festival grounds to forest soil.
